The Karen Lynch Story
Discover the remarkable journey of Karen Lynch, a former Pharmacy Technician who defied the odds to become a Fortune 500 CEO. 📈👩‍💼

In this inspiring video, we delve into the world of business, finance, and leadership, tracing Karen Lynch's path from her early days in the healthcare industry to her current role as a visionary leader on Wall Street. 💼📰

Amidst the challenges brought by the pandemic, Karen Lynch's strategic foresight and unwavering determination led CVS Health through uncharted territories, addressing the crisis head-on and playing a pivotal role in vaccine distribution. 💪💉

Join us as we explore her candid insights in an exclusive interview, where Karen Lynch discusses her journey, the future of healthcare, and her perspective on being a female CEO in the corporate world. 🎙️💡

From pharmacy shelves to the upper echelons of the Fortune 500, Karen Lynch's story is a testament to the power of ambition, resilience, and breaking barriers. 🌟🏢

For Karen Lynch, being recognized as one of the most powerful women in America was just the beginning of her journey to becoming an even more powerful woman. Since those early days, she has experienced many successes, but also faced plenty of challenges and disappointments. Here’s how Karen Lynch was able to turn her vision into reality by applying her own five principles on how to be a powerful woman.

Karen’s Story
Karen was born in Santa Monica, California. Her father’s death when she was only nine years old affected her tremendously. She moved with her mother and siblings to Whittier where they settled in a low-income neighborhood. They lived in government housing and Karen remembers how difficult it was on her mom; she took care of Karen and four other children, working two jobs most of her life. Even with all their challenges, Karen’s mother set an example for her children that showed them if you persevere and work hard you can be successful no matter what your circumstances are...

A Day in the Life of Karen Lynch
I wake up each morning and enjoy a cup of coffee on my terrace. My mornings are so peaceful, that is until I try to get our four-year-old son out of bed for school. He loves to stay in bed as long as possible which gives me time to read or write. For breakfast, I usually have oatmeal with blueberries and strawberries or protein pancakes with blackberries and whipped cream, paired with milk or my husband’s french press coffee, depending on how much time I have before work. When Alex goes off to daycare (he’s currently at home full-time), my daughter and I head out for her morning classes in Midtown East.Tips from Karen on How to Stand Out as an Influencer in the Digital World
Own Your Power! I don’t feel like it’s about more experience or being older. It’s about your point of view, it’s about you as an individual owning your power and saying what I have to say matters. Do we ever really listen when someone is just telling us facts? Maybe in school. But in life, people want experience and they want genuine—and that comes from how you deliver it and how authentic you are. If people can see through something or if they know that what I am saying is not coming from my heart, then I think they will lose respect for me no matter what my resume looks like.

How to Become a Powerful Woman Like Karen
Karen was only one of four women in her senior year of high school. She had goals and dreams, but they were considered pretty far-fetched by all of her peers. She graduated and went on to college; while she didn’t have big ambitions at first, she was majoring in business management because it just made sense. On top of that, she worked full-time for some extra cash, so she was able to get an apartment with three other girls after graduation. It wasn’t long before Karen realized that she didn’t like where things were going—she wanted more from life than just a 9-to-5 desk job or getting married and having kids.

Lessons Learned from Karen on Anxiety While Traveling

snippet.title: A Conversation with Karen Lynch, CEO, CVS Health
snippet.description:
November 7, 2023

Karen S. Lynch, President and CEO of CVS Health, leads more than 300,000 employees who are passionate about delivering superior health experiences for consumers by improving health outcomes, broadening access to care, and lowering costs. Under Karen’s leadership, CVS Health touches the lives of more than 100 million people through its health care and pharmacy benefits management businesses and presence in over 9,000 community health destinations across the United States.

Join the Exec Club's conversation with Karen S. Lynch and Mike Pykosz, CEO of Oak Street Health. Oak Street Health was recently acquired by CVS Health and helps advance the company’s commitment to broadening access to care, with a focus on underserved communities. During this event, Karen will reflect on how CVS Health is redefining health care, using technology to advance consumer health, and key leadership lessons from her more than three-decade career.

snippet.title: Artificial Intelligence: Advancements, Challenges, and Opportunities  │ The Exchange: Episode 14
snippet.description:
In this week's episode of #TheExchange by Greenbook, Karen Lynch and Lenny Murphy explored various news topics surrounding #AI. The New York Times featured an article about the rise of #AIAgents, using Pathlight's work as an example of how they're being utilized to create "insight streams" and the role of #marketresearch in unifying information. They also delved into the latest advancements in #CognitiveAI, as discussed in an article by AI Munch, which highlighted its potential in driving #decisionmaking, #insights, and #cognition. Additionally, we discussed the legal challenges faced by Stability AI regarding #copyright issues with #Midjourney, #DALL-E, and other image generators. They also touched upon the increasing number of legislation, lawsuits, and policies that are set to reshape the AI landscape. Finally, they emphasized the opportunities for #MRX in this evolving environment and the importance of embracing our role as "Keepers of the Why" and utilizing #smalldata training sets.

snippet.title: The Industry Needs to Address The Issues That Impact Sample Quality│The Exchange: Episode 17
snippet.description:
In this week’s edition of #TheExchange by Greenbook Karen Lynch and Lenny Murphy followed up on their “homework” from last week by summarizing the recent #BotGate panel discussion hosted by Kerry Hecht of Echo Market Research. The key takeaway: the industry needs to address the technological, engagement, and economic issues that impact #samplequality to fix the problem.

Then as another follow-up from last week they dived more deeply into the implications of the launch of Humane Pin, a new mobile technology that has the potential to be a challenger to the screen form factor. If it catches on, voice-driven functionality will drive #marketresearch to adapt to the new form factor across all methodologies, so we need to start thinking about it. On a related note, OpenAI had their big developer event this week and announced a ton of new stuff, including a foundational new tech ecosystem with an app store, new developer tools, and even more advanced capabilities. Of course, along with the tsunami of #AI news, this week Microsoft, Google, and OpenAI announced liability shields in front of all their developers to protect them from copyright and IP issues.

To tie all the AI news together they covered the new CB Insights “AI Bible”, a comprehensive review of the entire market, players, use cases and financials. It’s pretty amazing.

Finally they covered new research by StrawberryFrog and Dynata on the core driver of US #employee happiness, engagement and retention: meaningful work. The bottom line is that younger generations may have different priorities and motivations than previous generations, and that companies need to pay attention to the purpose of their work.

snippet.title: Survey Participation and Incentives │The Exchange: Episode 19
snippet.description:
In this episode of The Exchange, Karen Lynch and Lenny Murphy discussed various aspects of the market research industry. They began by addressing the recent changes at OpenAI, emphasizing the transformative potential of AI in market research. Privacy concerns and the role of data collection in AI training sets were highlighted, along with examples of innovative AI use in companies.

The second section focused on survey participation and incentives, stressing the need to make surveys more rewarding for participants. The discussion extended to the challenges of engaging consumers in a world of generative AI and a fragmented content ecosystem. They urged a reevaluation of participant rewards and discussed the potential disruption in the market research industry.

Overall, the episode underscored the significance of AI, proper incentives, and adapting to industry changes for market research professionals.

snippet.title: Inside the Acquisition: Signify Health’s CEO Talks About Becoming Part of the CVS Health Family
snippet.description:
What’s it like when CVS Health acquires your business for $8 billion dollars? Kyle Armbrester, CEO of Signify Health, says it “turbo charges your roadmap.” He’s here to share the inside story on the home health value-based care business that CVS Health just bought and how its go-to-market plan moving forward may not be exaaaactly what everyone would expect from CVS as one of healthcare’s most vertically-integrated organizations.... 

Spoiler alert. “We're not being integrated into Aetna,” explains Kyle. “We're a standalone, independent entity inside CVS that's going to continue to enjoy our multi-payer and multi-provider client base.” The phrase “payor agnostic” sums it up nicely, and Kyle explains exactly what this means, how the strategy is intended to play out in the market with Signify’s other provider and payor clients (including large national carriers like Humana), and why this approach is critical to Signify’s continued success – and CVS Health’s own efforts to lead – in the burgeoning home care market. 

Kyle says CVS Health’s CEO Karen Lynch has deemed it "a home care Renaissance" and he explains the macro forces he sees driving more healthcare into the home: from patients demanding better healthcare journeys to Medicare, Medicaid, and commercial payors making more assertive moves to remove cost and waste from the system by more frequently adopting total cost of care reimbursement models and re-thinking the home as a place for preventative care and screening. Adds Kyle, “I think CVS doing this deal is an acknowledgement that healthcare is being executed across a continuum. And while the retail segment is a vital part of that continuum, our hospital clients are a vital part of that continuum, the home is also something that's been overlooked for quite some time.”

So, what does this next-gen, screening-focused, home health model that Signify Health has brought into nearly 3 million homes this year look like? And, how does it bridge the gap between payor programs and its network of more than 200 health system partners? We get into all the parts and pieces of Signify’s model and its tech underpinnings, what fires Kyle up most about the potential of adding new medication management services thanks to CVS Pharmacy’s expertise, and, most importantly, gossip away about what will happen to Signify when CVS Health closes on its acquisition of Medicare-focused primary care provider Oak Street Health this summer.

*** 
Jessica DaMassa, the emerging ‘It girl’ of health tech interviewing, chats it up with the ‘who’s who’ of the health tech and healthcare innovation set on 'WTF Health - What's the Future, Health?' Catch 100's of interviews with leading health tech startups and the VC investors, health insurance companies, big pharma co's, and hospital systems helping bring their new ideas into the healthcare establishment. From AI and Big Data to digital health, virtual care, telehealth, digital therapeutics, payment model innovation, and investing, Jessica helps you spot the trends and figure out what’s next. 

Subscribe to WTF Health’s YouTube Channel: https://www.youtube.com/wtfhealth 
Follow Jess DaMassa on Twitter: https://twitter.com/jessdamassa
Visit WTF Health: https://www.wtf.health

snippet.title: Why MRX Pros Need to Pay Attention!  │The Exchange: Episode 21
snippet.description:
On Episode 21 of The Exchange Karen Lynch and Lenny Murphy covered the QRCA 2024 Naomi Henderson Legacy Award Winners, whether #AI is ready to replace moderators via a review of ResearchGOAT (an awesome new entrant into #ResTech!), the dawn of #brainoware, the "Inevitables of 2024" by Marketing Futures, the importance of #audio content based on a survey from Pocket FM, the latest from the AI Ethics Council, the launch of Salesforce to integrate data streams via AI, and a new report on #ConversationalAI by Aragon Research. As usual we tied it all together, focused on why #MRX pros need to pay attention, and had some fun while we were at it.

Shout-outs went to Buc-ee's, Ltd., Kerry Hecht, the late, great Naomi Henderson and Bill Weylock, Remesh, Recollective, Indiana University - Kelley School of Business, Dave McCaughan, E. David Zotter, Ipsos,  Google and even #Thanos (minus the #InfinityGauntlet).

Next week we'll go over our individual top 5 stories of the year before taking off for the holidays, so be sure to use the time to catch up on our past episodes as we hot the ground running in what is sure to be an exciting and disruptive 2024!

snippet.title: Muslim student denied diploma on stage over alleged rule breach
snippet.description:
Recent Philadelphia High School for Girls graduate Hafsah Abdur-Rahman claims she was unfairly denied her diploma on stage during the graduation ceremony on 9 June. The incident occurred after Abdur-Rahman's celebratory dance elicited laughter from the audience, apparently in violation of a strict no-cheering rule set by the principal.

Abdur-Rahman expressed deep humiliation and distress over the event, asserting that her principal, Lisa Mesi, "stole that moment from me". The 17-year-old student shared that the dance was not only a personal celebration but also a tribute to her sister, tragically killed at the age of 14. "I will never get that moment again," said a tearful Abdur-Rahman.

The School District of Philadelphia issued an apology, stating, "The District does not condone the withholding of earned diplomas based on family members cheering for their graduates." They also pledged to investigate the incident to prevent such occurrences in the future. Meanwhile, Abdur-Rahman and her mother have called for a revision of the school's traditions and rules in light of the incident.
